questions.stem: A topological space $X$ is called connected when which of the following holds?
options: Every continuous function on $X$ is constant.
$X$ cannot be written as the union of two disjoint nonempty open sets.
questions.explanation: Connectedness means there is no separation of $X$ into two disjoint nonempty open sets. Equivalently, the only subsets of $X$ that are both open and closed (clopen) are $\emptyset$ and $X$ itself.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A separation of a space $X$ is a pair of sets $U, V$ with which properties?
options: $U, V$ closed, with $U \cup V = X$ and $U \subseteq V$.
$U, V$ open, both possibly empty, with $U \cap V = X$.
$U, V$ disjoint open sets with $U \cup V = \emptyset$.
$U, V$ nonempty disjoint open sets with $U \cup V = X$.
questions.explanation: A separation consists of two nonempty, disjoint open sets whose union is all of $X$. A space is connected precisely when no such separation exists.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A space $X$ is connected if and only if its only clopen (simultaneously open and closed) subsets are which?
questions.explanation: Connectedness is equivalent to the statement that the only subsets that are both open and closed are $\emptyset$ and $X$. A nontrivial clopen set $A$ would give the separation $A$ and $X \setminus A$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which subsets of the real line $\mathbb{R}$ (with the standard topology) are exactly the connected ones?
options: The intervals (including rays, single points, and all of $\mathbb{R}$).
questions.explanation: A subset of $\mathbb{R}$ is connected if and only if it is an interval (a convex set): possibly a point, a bounded or unbounded interval, or all of $\mathbb{R}$. Disconnected subsets always have a gap that yields a separation.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: If $f : X \to Y$ is continuous and $X$ is connected, what can be said about the image $f(X)$?
options: It must be a single point.
questions.explanation: The continuous image of a connected space is connected. A separation of $f(X)$ would pull back via $f^{-1}$ to a separation of $X$, contradicting connectedness. The image need not be open or closed.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The Intermediate Value Theorem, viewed topologically, follows from which fact?
options: Every bounded sequence has a convergent subsequence.
A continuous bijection is a homeomorphism.
A continuous real-valued function on a connected space has connected image, hence takes all intermediate values.
questions.explanation: Since $[a,b]$ is connected and $f$ is continuous, $f([a,b])$ is a connected subset of $\mathbb{R}$, i.e. an interval. An interval containing $f(a)$ and $f(b)$ contains every value between them, which is exactly the Intermediate Value Theorem.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Suppose a collection of connected subspaces of $X$ all share a common point $p$. What is true of their union?
options: The union is connected only if there are finitely many.
The union is a single point.
questions.explanation: A union of connected sets with a common point is connected: any separation of the union would separate at least one of the connected pieces (since each piece lies in one part of the separation, and they all contain the shared point, forcing them into the same part).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: If $A$ is a connected subset of $X$ and $A \subseteq B \subseteq \overline{A}$, what can we conclude about $B$?
questions.explanation: Any set between a connected set and its closure is connected. In particular the closure $\overline{A}$ of a connected set is connected. Adding limit points cannot create a separation.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A connected component of a point $x$ in $X$ is which set?
options: The largest connected subset of $X$ that contains $x$.
The set of all points within distance $1$ of $x$.
questions.explanation: The connected component of $x$ is the union of all connected subsets containing $x$, which is the largest connected set containing $x$. Components partition the space and are always closed.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: How many connected components does the subspace $X = [0, 1] \cup [2, 3]$ of $\mathbb{R}$ have?
questions.explanation: Each closed interval $[0,1]$ and $[2,3]$ is connected, and the gap $(1,2)$ separates them, so $X$ has exactly $2$ connected components.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: In the discrete topology on a set $X$ with more than one point, the connected components are which sets?
questions.explanation: In a discrete space every singleton is clopen, so no set with two or more points can be connected. Thus the connected components are exactly the singletons; the space is totally disconnected.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A space $X$ is path-connected when which condition holds?
options: Every two points lie in a common open set.
For any two points $a, b \in X$ there is a continuous path $\gamma : [0,1] \to X$ with $\gamma(0) = a$, $\gamma(1) = b$.
questions.explanation: Path-connectedness requires a continuous path in $X$ joining any two points. Path-connected spaces are always connected, though the converse can fail (the topologist's sine curve is the standard counterexample).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: What is the relationship between path-connectedness and connectedness?
options: They are equivalent for all spaces.
Connected implies path-connected, but not conversely.
Path-connected implies connected, but not conversely.
questions.explanation: Every path-connected space is connected (a path image is connected, and paths from a base point cover the space with a common point). The converse fails: the topologist's sine curve is connected but not path-connected.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the subspace $\mathbb{Q}$ of rational numbers (standard topology) connected?
options: Yes, because it is dense in $\mathbb{R}$.
It is connected only as a subset of $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.explanation: Between any two rationals there is an irrational $r$, and the sets $\{q \in \mathbb{Q} : q < r\}$ and $\{q \in \mathbb{Q} : q > r\}$ form a separation. Hence $\mathbb{Q}$ is totally disconnected: its only connected subsets are single points.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the real line $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology connected?
options: Yes, since $\mathbb{R}$ is an interval and intervals are connected.
No, the rationals and irrationals separate it.
Only the bounded intervals are connected.
questions.explanation: The whole line $\mathbb{R}$ is an interval, and intervals in $\mathbb{R}$ are connected. Unboundedness does not affect connectedness, and the rationals/irrationals are not open, so they do not form a separation.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: A space in which every connected component is a single point is called which of the following?
questions.explanation: A space whose components are all singletons is totally disconnected. Examples include any discrete space and the rationals $\mathbb{Q}$ with the subspace topology.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Is the product $X \times Y$ of two connected spaces (with the product topology) connected?
options: Yes, a product of connected spaces is connected.
Only if one factor is a point.
questions.explanation: The product of connected spaces is connected. One sees this by covering $X \times Y$ with connected slices ($\{x\} \times Y$ and $X \times \{y\}$) sharing common points, whose union is connected and exhausts the product.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Connectedness is which kind of property?
options: A metric property that depends on distances.
A property only finite spaces can have.
A property of the underlying set alone.
A topological property, preserved by homeomorphisms.
questions.explanation: Connectedness is defined purely in terms of open sets, so it is a topological invariant: if $X$ is connected and $X \cong Y$, then $Y$ is connected. It does not depend on any metric.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: How many connected components does a discrete space with exactly $5$ points have?
questions.explanation: In a discrete space every singleton is clopen, so each of the $5$ points is its own component. Hence there are $5$ connected components.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Consider $\mathbb{R}$ with the cofinite topology (open sets are $\emptyset$ and the cofinite sets). Is this space connected?
options: Yes, because two nonempty open sets always intersect, so no separation exists.
Only the finite subsets are connected.
questions.explanation: In the cofinite topology on an infinite set, any two nonempty open sets have cofinite complements, so their intersection is cofinite and nonempty. Two disjoint nonempty open sets cannot exist, so there is no separation and the space is connected.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which statement about the closure of a connected set $A$ in a space $X$ is correct?
options: $\overline{A}$ is connected only if $A$ is closed.
questions.explanation: The closure of a connected set is connected, since $A \subseteq \overline{A} \subseteq \overline{A}$ and any set sandwiched between a connected set and its closure is connected. Adjoining limit points cannot disconnect the set.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Let $X = \{a, b, c\}$ carry the indiscrete topology $\{\emptyset, X\}$. Is $X$ connected?
options: No, the points $a, b, c$ separate it.
It depends on the points chosen.
Yes, the only clopen sets are $\emptyset$ and $X$.
questions.explanation: In the indiscrete topology the only open sets are $\emptyset$ and $X$, so the only clopen sets are $\emptyset$ and $X$. There is no separation, so the space is connected (it has a single component).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: The subspace $\{0\} \cup \{1/n : n = 1, 2, 3, \dots\}$ of $\mathbb{R}$ has how many connected components?
options: Infinitely many (each point is its own component).
questions.explanation: Each point of this set is isolated except possibly $0$, but $0$ is also its own component since the set is totally disconnected (between any two of its points lies a gap). With infinitely many points, there are infinitely many components, one per point.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Which of the following spaces is connected?
options: The unit circle $S^1$ in the plane.
The set $\{1, 2, 3\}$ with the discrete topology.
The integers $\mathbb{Z}$ as a subspace of $\mathbb{R}$.
questions.explanation: The circle $S^1$ is the continuous image of the connected interval $[0,1]$ under $t \mapsto (\cos 2\pi t, \sin 2\pi t)$, hence connected. The discrete spaces and $\mathbb{Z}$ are totally disconnected (each point is clopen).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Consider the subspace $X = [0, 1] \cup (2, 3) \cup \{5\}$ of $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology. Count its connected components and identify which single component is just one point.
questions.solution: Each maximal interval piece is connected, and the gaps between them give separations, so the pieces $[0,1]$, $(2,3)$, and $\{5\}$ are the components. The total number of connected components is {{1}}. Of these, the number of components that consist of a single point is {{2}}, namely the isolated point. The piece $(2,3)$, being an interval, {{3}} connected.
questions.explanation: The three maximal interval pieces $[0,1]$, $(2,3)$, $\{5\}$ are the connected components, so there are $3$. Exactly $1$ of them, $\{5\}$, is a single point. The interval $(2,3)$ is connected since intervals in $\mathbb{R}$ are connected.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Count components of a finite discrete space, then apply the continuous-image principle. Take the discrete space on $4$ points.
questions.solution: In a discrete space every singleton is clopen, so a set with two or more points is never connected. For a discrete space on $4$ points the number of connected components is {{1}}, one per point. Because a continuous image of a connected space is connected, the image of a connected space under a continuous map {{2}} be disconnected.
questions.explanation: A discrete $4$-point space has $4$ singleton components. Since continuous images of connected spaces are connected, such an image cannot be disconnected.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Use the Intermediate Value Theorem as a connectedness statement. Let $f : [0, 1] \to \mathbb{R}$ be continuous with $f(0) = -2$ and $f(1) = 3$. Reason about values attained.
questions.solution: Since $[0,1]$ is connected and $f$ is continuous, the image $f([0,1])$ is a connected subset of $\mathbb{R}$, that is, an interval. This interval contains both $-2$ and $3$, so it contains every value between them. In particular the equation $f(x) = 0$ {{1}} a solution in $[0,1]$. The number of the two endpoint values, $-2$ and $3$, that are negative is {{2}}.
questions.explanation: The image is an interval containing $-2$ and $3$, hence containing $0$; so $f(x) = 0$ has a solution. Among the two endpoint values, only $-2$ is negative, so the count is $1$.
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology

questions.stem: Compare the rationals $\mathbb{Q}$ and the full line $\mathbb{R}$ as subspaces under the standard topology, focusing on connectedness.
questions.solution: The line $\mathbb{R}$ is an interval, so it {{1}} connected. By contrast, between any two rationals lies an irrational that splits $\mathbb{Q}$ into two disjoint nonempty relatively open pieces, so $\mathbb{Q}$ is {{2}}. The number of points in the largest connected subset of $\mathbb{Q}$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: $\mathbb{R}$ is connected (an interval). $\mathbb{Q}$ is totally disconnected because an irrational cut separates it, so its largest connected subset is a single point ($1$ point).
questions.source: in the style of Munkres, Topology
